# Preface {.unnumbered}

This dissertation comprises four papers and one methodological supplement,
produced between 2025 and 2026 as part of the Dark Forest research programme.
Each paper was written as a self-contained contribution to *Artificial Life*,
but together they form a cumulative argument about how functional complexity
emerges from structurally simple substrates.

The programme proceeds through five stages: individuation (Paper 1),
coexistence (Paper 2), the self-sealing barrier (Paper 3), metaplastic
differentiation (Paper 4), and the methodological reckoning that revealed a
pruning confound and strengthened the programme's conclusions (Paper 4S).

The chapters that follow provide the connecting narrative. Chapter 1
introduces the research question and philosophical framework. Chapter 2
describes the shared computational methods. Chapters 3--7 present the five
papers with contextual commentary. Chapter 8 synthesises the cross-cutting
themes, and Chapter 9 concludes with directions for future work.

All simulation code, experimental data, and analysis scripts are available
in the four programme repositories. The experimental programme comprises
approximately 15,000 simulation jobs producing over 3 million session-level
observations, executed on a distributed Ray cluster.
